question: The counseling sessions in this benchmark likely reflect specific cultural patterns around how clients in India express distress — for example, somatization of psychological symptoms, family-system framing of problems, stigma-shaped disclosure patterns, or caste/religion-inflected life stressors. Do the transcripts your system will process follow similar disclosure patterns, or do you also serve practitioners in other Global South contexts (e.g., Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, Latin America) where these cultural presentations differ meaningfully?

The benchmark focuses on three counseling components as summarization targets. In your practitioners' actual workflows, are there session elements they critically need captured that may not map onto those three components — for example, risk assessments, homework assignments, medication discussions, culturally-specific coping strategies (religious/spiritual framing, family interventions), or referral decisions? What does a 'complete' clinical note look like for your users?

The benchmark's quality judgments were verified by mental health professionals — but practitioner norms for what counts as a good session summary can vary across clinical traditions and regional contexts. Are your users trained in Western CBT/psychodynamic frameworks, or do they practice integrative or culturally-adapted modalities (e.g., Yoga-based therapy, family systems approaches common in South Asian contexts)? Would their criteria for a high-quality summary diverge from the benchmark annotators' judgments?

Your target users are described as Hindi-English bilingual practitioners. Will the session transcripts they feed into the system be fully in English, or will they include code-switched Hindi-English dialogue, transliterated Hindi, or regional language insertions (e.g., Tamil, Bengali, Telugu)? If transcripts contain non-English content, does the system need to handle or summarize those segments accurately?
